HEARING RE: DEFENDANT UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s Application For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Uber Technologies, Inc.'s motion for good faith settlement determination is granted.

Plaintiff rode in a Flywheel taxi that ran a red light, causing collisions with two separate Uber drivers. One got out on summary judgment. The other Uber driver was allegedly in a transit lane, so had some potential liability, which he and Uber have now settled with plaintiff after negotiations by experienced counsel.

Once, as here, a settlor makes a preliminary showing, a non-settling party has the burden of demonstrating that a settlement is "so far 'out of the ballpark'" as to be inconsistent with the law's equitable objectives. (Tech-Bilt, Inc. v. Woodward-Clyde & Associates (1985) 38 Cal.3d 488, 499-500.)

The taxi defendants' lead argument is that the settlement "was obtained by way of collusion," but the assertion is supported by no evidence. Why plaintiff would take less from Uber than she could get is not explained. The taxi defendants also note that plaintiff once demanded $22 million in damages. That was puffery; the taxi defendants certainly adduce no evidence to the contrary. I have reviewed the Tech-Bilt factors and find this settlement in good faith.

Order signed.
